channel.title: News Articles on IQSS Clone - Harvard Institute for Quantitative Social Science

item.title: 2023 OpenDP Community Meeting Recap

item.description: Recap of the 2024 OpenDP meeting held at Harvard.

item.title: A focus on flourishing: Newest IQSS program
item.description: Combining social science and philosophy for human flourishing.

item.title: A Love of Learning and Helping Others Succeed: Wendy Guan Retires
item.description:
By Colleen Walsh

A map covered in red dots hangs on Wendy Guan’s office wall. The dense rosy points obscure what lies beneath until a closer look reveals Italy, Sicily, and the tip of Tunisia.

Those vibrant points enabled Harvard’s Francis Goelet Professor of Medieval History Michael McCormick to connect the dots. He had turned to Guan and her team at Harvard’s IQSS Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA) to help track the path of the Plague of Justinian (541–ca. 750 CE) for his medieval history class.

item.title: A New Industry-Academic Initiative at IQSS
item.description: New initiative to transform academic research into market-ready ventures.

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Adam Stefkovics
item.description: Visiting researcher with a Fulbright scholarship.

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Jacob Brown
item.description:
Jacob is a resident graduate student at IQSS who is graduating this spring with a PhD in Government and Social Policy. If you see him around CGIS, be sure to wish him congratulations and good luck!

What is the focus of your work?

“Political geography and political behavior in American politics.”

What’s something you like about IQSS?

“The grad students have semi-regular dinners that are a lot of fun.”

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Maria Ballesteros
item.description: Maria is a graduate student and resident affiliate at IQSS.

item.title: Affiliate Spotlight: Sascha Riaz
item.description: Harvard Government PhD student at IQSS.

item.title: AI leaps from math dunce to whiz
item.description: IQSS affiliate Michael Brenner on AI advancements in mathematics.

item.title: Al Roth Receives Nobel Prize in Economics
item.description: IQSS affiliate awarded the 2012 Economics Nobel.

item.title: An Amazing 2025 Dataverse Community Meeting
item.description:
The IQSS Dataverse Core team recently returned from the 2025 Dataverse Community Meeting, which was hosted by the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. The event represented the culmination of two and a half years of intensive development work, resulting in several major milestones for the Dataverse Project.

Key Highlights

  • A new React-based frontend, expected to be available in production in July 2025
  • The launch of Dataverse Hub and Dataverse Marketplace
  • Significant advancements in AI readiness, including full support for the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Croissant-ML metadata schema. Dataverse was selected as one of the data repositories for the NeurIPS25 conference, described as “the most important AI conference of the year”
  • AutoSage — an AI Metadata helper built on open source large language models, fully trained at IQSS on the complete Harvard Dataverse data, along with the forthcoming Dataverse Knowledge Graph
  • A powerful External Search Tool that uses natural language queries to interrogate the Dataverse repository alongside traditional keyword and facet-based search
  • New integration with high performance computing infrastructures, including the internal FAS-RC cluster, via a tool called LOOP that connects OpenOnDemand and Dataverse seamlessly (a collaboration between the Dataverse and HPC teams at IQSS)
  • New Large Data Support services deployed at scale

Community Growth

The Dataverse community continues to expand, with worldwide installations growing from 70 in 2022 to over 135 today. The meeting showcased contributions from institutions and developers across the globe.

item.title: Bob Treacy: From the Factory Floor to Harvard and 30 Years of Java
item.description: Bob Treacy, Principal Software Architect on the Dataverse Project, discusses his path into software development, Javas evolution over 30 years, and the role of AI in research software.

item.title: Building terraFlow: How an Idea in the Lab Grew Into a Venture-Backed Startup
item.description: Dan Freeman, a Harvard PhD student in Biology and Biomedical Sciences, had prior experience with single-cell technology and pharma trials. Convinced there was a better approach, he began experimenting with artificial intelligence and arrived at his breakthrough concept. His vision involved using AI to accelerate drug discovery, and he had developed an early prototype. What he needed was guidance on commercialization—identifying customers, refining his pitch, and building a business plan. The IQSS Startup Foundry provided that support.

item.description: Submissions are open for the Center for Geographic Analysis Conference, celebrating the CGAs 20th anniversary with a past, present, and future theme.

item.title: Class of 2026 at IQSS: Whats Next?
item.description:
The IQSS community includes affiliated graduate students across Harvard University engaged in social science research. As the academic year ends, some graduate students complete their programs and move on. Below are updates from graduating affiliates continuing their work beyond Harvard.

Dorothy Manevich (PhD, Government, American Politics)

Dorothy is joining the national political department of the United Autoworkers Union (UAW).

Jeremiah Cha (PhD, Government)

In the fall, Jeremiah will join the Center for the Study of Democratic Politics at Princeton University.

item.title: Community Announcement: New Space Arrangements at IQSS
item.description: New space arrangements following the pandemic.

item.title: Crime and Police Reform In America—Does Politics Matter?
item.description:
by Courtney Hayes

On May 25, 2020, a Minneapolis police officer killed George Floyd by kneeling on his neck for almost 10 minutes. Floyd’s death sparked the largest mass protest movement in U.S. history and a national reckoning on race and police reform.

In the fall of 2020, pressure to act was intensifying. Protestors called for radical reform while crime rates spiked. Between 2019 and 2020, the U.S. murder rate rose 30%—the largest single-year increase in more than a century. As election season approached, former President Donald Trump began blaming Democratic mayors.

item.title: Crunching Data on American Elections: The CCES
item.description: Compiling data about who votes in America and why.

item.title: Cynthia Dwork wins Knuth Prize
item.description: Outstanding contributions to computer science.

item.title: Data Highway: A Trailblazing Journey into Trucking
item.description:
by Courtney Hayes

As highways become a new digital landscape where every truck serves as a node and every delivery a connection, a Harvard PhD-turned-entrepreneur is helping build tomorrow’s transportation infrastructure. IQSS affiliate Jinyan Zang, founder and COO of Axle, has created a data platform that seeks to transform trucking logistics.

The trucking industry today is massive. With almost four million tractor-trailers on US highways, trucking accounts for over 70% of the nation’s freight. The internet now connects every truck in the United States with the potential to track fuel levels, speed, location, and more. But the industry is highly fragmented, with thousands of brokers and dispatchers working around the clock to manage siloed data streams. As a result, up to 30% of all vehicles are driving empty. Zang notes: “That’s burning a ton of diesel without actually doing any work. That’s money out of the driver’s and shipper’s pocket.”

item.title: Following in Ancient Footsteps: Jeff Blossom Maps an Explorer Walk
item.description: Mapping Paul Salopek Out of Eden Walk.

item.title: Forgotten Graves: A New Lens on Colonial Slavery
item.description:
by Courtney Hayes

Jason Ur, Stephen Phillips Professor of Archaeology and Ethnology and IQSS affiliate, is working to unearth histories long hidden in Cambridge. Known for pioneering archaeological work in the Middle East, Ur has turned his attention to Cambridge’s Old Burying Ground, where questions about segregation and slavery have remained buried for centuries. Using drones, ground-penetrating radar, and a geospatial database, he is re-mapping the past and challenging how we understand it.

item.title: From Harvard Research to National Security Innovation: The Story of Thresher
item.description:
In 2015, Weatherhead University Professor Gary King co-founded Thresher, a company born from his academic research at Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS).

Thresher’s origins trace back to two groundbreaking research programs. The first focused on censorship and fabrication of Chinese social media posts, conducted with then graduate students Patrick Lam (now at Airbnb), Jennifer Pan (now professor at Stanford), and Molly Roberts (now professor at UCSD). While analyzing data from an earlier startup, Crimson Hexagon (now Brandwatch), King and his collaborators discovered a method to download Chinese social media content before it could be censored by the government. This serendipitous finding gave them access to the entire corpus of posts Chinese citizens were not allowed to read, becoming the foundation for deeper analysis of online information manipulation.

item.title: From NASA to the Enterprise: LISH Expands Programs
item.description: Laboratory for Innovation Science experienced unprecedented growth.

item.title: From the Directors: A Fall Update
item.description:
This last year has brought a lot of exciting and transformative advancements at IQSS and across the social sciences. Here are a few highlights.

Dataverse Project

The Dataverse Project continued its community growth worldwide, reaching 100 installations, thousands of dataverses, and millions of dataset downloads. IQSS is undertaking a major restructuring of the Dataverse UI as a “single-page application,” which will enable custom UIs, integration with other applications, and make novel statistical innovations instantly available to users. The Dataverse Project is also part of an effort by the Office of Data Science Strategy (ODSS) at the NIH to launch a new data curation, sharing, and interoperability initiative across repositories. The team is also testing experimental code leveraging large language model (LLM) applications within Dataverse, allowing users to interact with and ask questions about Dataverse conversationally.

item.title: From the Directors: An Update on Dataverse
item.description: Deep dives into maximizing impact.

item.title: Future of Media Project: Who Owns Canadian News Media?
item.description: IQSS fellow published a Canadian Media Ownership Index.

item.description: A new model for academic collaboration with industry.

item.title: Gary King Receives 2019 Excellence in Mentoring Award
item.description: Society for Political Methodology award.

item.title: Gary King: A Welcome Back Update
item.description:
Dear: IQSS Staff and Affiliates
From: Gary King, Director IQSS
Subj: A welcome back update
Date: 8/19/2021

King opens by noting the IQSS community has been away from CGIS for nearly two years, with exceptions for the Client Support Services team and others. He announces the official return the week of August 23rd.

He describes the pre-pandemic intellectual community at IQSS as unique “not only among Harvard groups, but throughout academia” and outlines goals to preserve the virtual community, reconstitute the physical one, and build on lessons learned about workplace flexibility.

item.title: In touch with our emotions, finally
item.description: Jennifer Lerner examines how emotion and gender factor into decision making.

item.title: Introducing Extraordinary Claims, Extraordinary Evidence
item.description:
by Elizabeth Salazar

The Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS) launched a program offering Harvard faculty exceptional help in providing extraordinary evidence for their extraordinary claims. Called Extraordinary Claims, Extraordinary Evidence (ECEE), the program aims to catalyze rigorous research that would shift dominant scientific positions significantly.

The program recognizes that many extraordinary claims are “explicitly contrarian positions” that, if validated, reveal flawed assumptions or unsupported conclusions in existing research. Others address subjects not actively studied—topics scholars incorrectly assume are understood or consider unworthy of investigation—yet which hold potential for major shifts in a field’s dominant view.

item.title: Introducing Version 0.4 of the OpenDP Library
item.description: New measurements and floating point improvements.

item.title: IQSS Affiliate-Led HDSI Receives Sloan Grant
item.description: A $1 million grant for causal inference and machine learning.

item.title: IQSS and Collaborators Announce How We Feel Project
item.description: Addressing the gap in recorded COVID-19 cases.

item.title: IQSS Celebrates 20 years
item.description:
The year 2025 marks the 20th anniversary of the Institute for Quantitative Social Science at Harvard University. The milestone was celebrated at the Center for Geographic and International Studies (CGIS) with faculty, staff, and students from across the IQSS community who work to advance research at Harvard and other institutions.

Over two decades, IQSS has developed into a hub where leading minds in data and social science collaborate on solutions to pressing societal issues. As the institute marks this milestone and reflects on its accomplishments, projects, and innovations, it is also looking ahead—including new programs and partnerships that will launch at IQSS.

item.title: IQSS Co-Hosts Comparative Politics and Formal Theory Conference
item.description: The 8th CPFT Conference at Harvard.

item.description: The first annual Dataverse Community Meeting.

item.title: IQSS Honors Staff Service Milestones
item.description:
The Institute for Quantitative Social Science recently gathered to celebrate staff members who have reached important service milestones. At IQSS they value their community, and their dedicated staff is at the heart of that community. This annual recognition honors colleagues who in 2025 reached a work anniversary of 10 years or more, in 5-year increments. Their long-term commitment, talent, and collaboration make our work possible.

This year’s IQSS Service Award recipients are:

item.title: IQSS launches Climate Justice Design Fellowship program
item.description: Supporting climate justice through data science and design.

item.title: IQSS launches Sid cloud research software
item.description: Cloud agnostic multi-user research computing platform.

item.title: IQSS Welcomes Dr. Stefano Iacus
item.description: Dr. Iacus joins as Senior Research Scientist.

item.title: IQSS Welcomes New Executive Director Elizabeth Jackson
item.description:
IQSS announced the arrival of its new Executive Director, Elizabeth Jackson, who comes from Greater Portland Health in Maine and from Harvard’s T.H. Chan School of Public Health’s Department of Global Health and Population.

Jackson has built a career aligned with IQSS’s mission. She most recently served as Chief Administrative Officer of The Greater Portland Health Center and has engaged in health system and community strengthening work with partners across Africa, Asia, and Latin America. She has also led trauma-informed resilience trainings.

item.title: IQSS Welcomes WashU into Magaro Peer Pre-Review Consortium
item.description:
by Liz Salazar

The Institute for Quantitative Social Science at Harvard University is expanding the Alexander and Diviya Magaro Peer Pre-Review Program—a service designed to improve scholarship by speeding up the journal submission process—into a multi-institutional consortium. The Weidenbaum Center on the Economy, Government, and Public Policy at Washington University in St. Louis is the first affiliate to partner with IQSS, adding its own network of associate editors and peer reviewers to Harvard’s existing cohort. Washington University professors Gaetano Antinolfi and Ian Filmore will work with senior preceptor Chase Harrison at IQSS to coordinate the review process for submitted drafts.

item.title: Is AI dulling our minds?
item.description: IQSS faculty affiliate Dan Levy weighs in with other Harvard faculty on whether AI will help or harm critical thinking and its effect on the human mind.

item.title: Is the Two-Parent Family the Great Equalizer?
item.description: Examining whether marriage and the two-parent family serve as a great equalizer.

item.title: James Robins Recognized with COPSS Distinguished Achievement Award
item.description: James Robins receives the COPSS Distinguished Achievement Award.

item.description: Celebrating Jeffry Frieden and his 30 years at Harvard.

item.title: Ji Su Yoo Awarded P.D. Soros Fellowship
item.description: Ji Su Yoo named a 2020 Soros Fellow.

item.title: Join Dataverse for the GREI Collaborative Webinar Series
item.description: Repository resources and best practices for NIH-funded research.

item.title: Kenneth Shepsle Awarded William H. Riker Prize
item.description: Kenneth Shepsle received the 2023 William H. Riker prize in Political Science.

item.title: Kosuke Imai Returns to Harvard
item.description:
by Jonathan Colburn

Changing Seasons: Kosuke Imai Returns to Harvard to Expand the Reach of Quantitative Social Science at Harvard and Abroad

When he last occupied office space at Harvard, Kosuke Imai, IQSS’s newest resident faculty affiliate, had a decidedly different experience than he does in his current location at CGIS Knafel overlooking Cambridge Street.

“IQSS didn’t exist yet, but its predecessor was housed in a little yellow house where the Ukrainian Research Institute is,” Imai recalls. His graduate student office was upstairs in the attic, and demolition of the old building nearby meant that construction equipment would shake his entire workspace.

item.description: Latanya Sweeney to join FTC as Chief Technologist.

item.description: Incredible Women Advancing A.I. Research.

item.description: The China Biographical Database (CBDB) team redesigned their name indexing method to make text analysis research considerably faster.

item.title: Mapping Chinese History: The China Biographical Database
item.description: Cataloging individuals across millennia of Chinese society.

item.title: Melissa Dell Wins 2020 Clark Medal
item.description: Melissa Dell receives the John Bates Clark Medal.

item.title: Memorious: Building Infinite Memory with AI
item.description:
Forgetting is universal—conversations, names, moments all slip away. But what if it were optional? That’s the ambitious vision behind Memorious, a company founded by Spandan Madan and Gabriel Kreiman at Harvard. Their goal is to reinvent human memory using AI, building tools that allow people to recall experiences, conversations, and information instantly without searching or prompting.

From Research to Reality

The science powering Memorious originates from Gabriel’s lab at Harvard. As a Professor of Neuroscience and AI at Harvard Medical School, Gabriel has spent decades researching how the brain encodes and retrieves memory. His lab’s findings formed the technological foundation for the company. He partnered with Spandan Madan, a Harvard Computer Science PhD with extensive machine learning experience from Microsoft, Adobe, and IIT. Together they recognized the chance to merge neuroscience and AI to create what they term “Ambient Intelligence.”

item.title: Merce Crosas Departs to be Secretary of Open Government in Catalonia
item.description: After 15 years at Harvard, Crosas departs for Catalonia.

item.title: Mind2Market Demo Day
item.description: Harvard researchers including IQSS Startup Foundry teams pitch AI projects ready to become ventures and seeking partners.

item.title: NASA Tournament Lab Launches Asteroid Hunter Contest
item.description: Challenging competitors to develop better asteroid detection.

item.title: Nate Silver to Lead Political Analytics 2017
item.description: Second annual Political Analytics Conference at Harvard.

item.title: Neru Health - From Sleep Challenges to Scaling Chronic Care with AI
item.description:
When Neru Health first came to the IQSS Startup Foundry, they had a big idea: transform the way people solve sleep problems. Millions struggle with conditions like sleep apnea, and while treatments exist, staying consistent with therapy is notoriously difficult. Neru’s early vision centered on improving patient engagement and adherence to sleep therapy.

But as the team dug deeper during their time in the Foundry, they uncovered a bigger and more urgent opportunity. Durable Medical Equipment (DME) providers, responsible for supplying and supporting patients with CPAP machines and other critical devices, were stuck between trying to grow while maintaining excellent patient care and adherence to treatment. Staffing shortages, rising costs, and manual support processes left DMEs unable to keep up with patient needs.

item.title: New IQSS Web Site Launched!
item.description: Created through cross-team collaboration.

item.title: New R Package {clarify}
item.description:
Data Science Services at IQSS has released a new R package {clarify} to provide functionality previously provided by the {Zelig} package.

The package “uses simulation to make interpretable inferences about any quantity of interest from a wide variety of statistical models.” It restores and extends functionality that was previously available through the {Zelig} package for R and the Clarify package for Stata. The package supports many model classes from a variety of other R packages and offers methods for computing interpretable quantities from models fit to multiply imputed data.

item.title: Newspaper Endorsements and the Importance of Candidate Quality in Politics
item.description: Kevin DeLuca describes political endorsement data he collected.

item.title: Now Announcing the OpenDP Library 0.7
item.description: OpenDP Library 0.7 with architectural additions.

item.title: On data centers, local vibes matter more than electricity prices
item.description: A survey led by faculty affiliate Stephen Ansolabehere explores what Americans prioritize regarding large data centers opening in their communities. The research found that local community concerns matter more to residents than electricity pricing when it comes to data center siting decisions.

item.description: Groundwork for incorporating mathematical proofs of OpenDP code.

item.description: Politics and Computational Social Science conference.

item.title: Partisanship and pandemic preparedness in Indonesia
item.description: How partisan politics affect pandemic preparedness.

item.title: Peer Pre-Review is now rapidPeer
item.description:
IQSS has rebranded its Peer Pre Review service as rapidPeer.

The service addresses frustrations with “the speed and quality of your journal peer reviews” by offering free, double-blind peer reviews for research papers, grants, or preregistration plans within 30 days. Reviewers are field-specific experts from leading institutions who provide feedback on both quantitative and qualitative research.

For Harvard researchers, rapidPeer delivers “quick, actionable insights that help refine your work and expedite the publication process,” potentially saving months in the submission cycle. To date, the service has supported 140 scholars in maximizing their publishing success.

item.title: Pope Leo XIV names epidemiology professor to Vatican advisory group
item.description: Tyler VanderWeele, director of the Human Flourishing Program at IQSS, was appointed by Pope Leo XIV to the Pontifical Academy of Social Sciences.

item.title: Powerful computing in comfortable surroundings: the IQSS Computer Labs
item.description:
by Danielle Benaroche Gottesman

Nestled within Harvard’s Knafel Building—known primarily for government and international studies—is a high-end computing refuge. It’s always open and filled with cutting-edge technology and software.

“Most computer labs have gray walls and feel very plain,” says IQSS Computer Lab Manager Randy Kiessig. “We have a colorful atmosphere, beautiful wood desks, and the winter garden—which feels a lot like a coffee shop.”

Students have long benefited from on-campus computer labs, but pandemic-era lifestyle changes shifted user habits toward personal computer use in isolated spaces. The labs aim to meet the full spectrum of needs for Harvard students, staff, and faculty, offering both pleasing and practical environments for focused work.

item.title: Preparing the Next Steps and the Next Generation: Latanya Sweeney's Data Privacy Lab
item.description: Exploring digital information risks and the work of Latanya Sweeney’s labs.

item.title: Science needs contrarians, and contrarians need support
item.description: The ECEE program at IQSS supports Harvard faculty undertaking high-risk research that could provide novel insights by testing dominant views in social science.

item.title: Streamlining the Publication Process with Magaro Peer Pre-Review
item.description: Streamlining the traditional publishing process.

item.title: The art of the online interface
item.description:
By Colleen Walsh

New desktop application streamlines connection between IQSS’s Dataverse and Sid

A new application called LOOP, developed at Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS), aims to simplify the workflow for researchers who want to analyze data from the Harvard Dataverse using Sid—Harvard’s version of the open-source Open OnDemand dashboard that connects scholars with supercomputers.

Len Wisniewski, senior director of research computing at IQSS, leads the Sid team that co-designed the LOOP application with the Dataverse team. The software targets researchers across disciplines—from political science to psychology to the physical sciences.

item.title: The Community that Makes the Center: the Whiteboards of IQSS
item.description:
by Gary King

IQSS director Gary King reflects on the origins of the writing surfaces that cover the hallways of the Institute, as featured in a recent article by the Harvard Gazette.

When King started IQSS, he interviewed many current and former Harvard deans and presidents to understand what made the best centers succeed. He looked at places like the legendary Cavendish in London, Bell Labs, ISR, and others. Everyone he spoke with had a different story but arrived at the same conclusion. One person would emphasize great space, but then acknowledge that some great centers lacked great space. Others would stress funding, then concede it wasn’t really about money. But ultimately, every single person he consulted landed on the same answer: community. As King writes, “It is the intellectual community that makes the center.” He had a specific notion of how to build that community at IQSS, described in an article he wrote about IQSS, and notes that “this method wound up working great!”

item.title: The Enigma of the Undecided Voter
item.description:
by Vinay Mehra

Originally posted on Vinay Mehra’s Substack.

The article explores the conventional image of the undecided voter—the suburban mom or blue-collar worker who can’t choose between candidates—and argues this framing is far too narrow. Researchers at Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS) suggest that the firm distinction between “decided” and “undecided” voters may not actually exist.

The Real Swing

The article contends that so-called undecided voters aren’t a separate breed standing on a fence. Rather, they sit near the middle of the political spectrum, close to a threshold where political shifts make their vote changes more visible. The entire electorate sways together; those in the middle simply show the movement most clearly. Even solidly partisan areas experience shifts—just less obviously.

item.title: Tracking the American electorate: the Cooperative Election Study
item.description: Feature on the Cooperative Election Study supported by IQSS.

item.title: Travel Time Estimation for Geospatial Big Data
item.description: Drive time estimation in healthcare accessibility research.

item.title: Turns out two-parent households are no fix for racial inequality
item.description: IQSS affiliate Christina Crosss latest book critically examines the idea that a two-parent nuclear family is the simple solution to inequality.

item.description: Visit to Seoul National University for Dataverse talks and training.

item.title: Waves 1 and 2 of the Global Flourishing Study Are Now Open to the Public
item.description: The Global Flourishing Study dataset release includes data from roughly 200,000 participants across 22 countries, covering six domains of flourishing in a longitudinal design.

item.title: What science says about Moms happiness advice
item.description: IQSS affiliate Daniel Gilbert shared his mothers happiness advice at a lecture, explaining how scientific research on happiness supports conventional wisdom.

item.title: Why self-appraisals may not be best way to judge job performance
item.description: Faculty affiliate Iris Bohnet discusses her recent paper on worker performance ratings and biases affecting how employees are rated.
